raw_payload.author: saolivaresm
raw_payload.content:
Patrick Hughes makes a strong start on Netflix with War Machine, a film that seems straight out of the glorious eighties, but with all the visual power of 2026. It's science fiction, it's action, and above all, it's pure entertainment.

The premise works because it doesn't try to reinvent the wheel: it blends patriotism, testosterone, and a tough hero reminiscent of the genre's classics. Alan Ritchson shines as a ranger who must survive in natural settings so wild they become characters in their own right: rocks, trees, and rivers accompany every explosive sequence.

The best part is that Ritchson doesn't just rely on muscle; he brings dramatic nuances that give his mission emotional weight. This combination of drama, action, and science fiction makes the film a well-rounded pastime, the kind you can enjoy guilt-free.

Visually, it's a spectacle: a blockbuster that cries out for the big screen, even though Netflix has secured it as their big release for 2026. And yes, it's not perfect, but it doesn't need to be. What matters is that it gives us two hours of pure adrenaline and nostalgia, as if an '80s hero had traveled back in time to save us from the chaos of the future.

In short: War Machine is fun, explosive, and seductive. A film that delivers on its promise: to give us a great time.

raw_payload.author: eddie_baggins
raw_payload.content:
It's a relatively safe bet to suggest that Netflix's latest sci-fi actioner isn't going to be competing for any Oscars come this time next year, but for a film that promises us the chance to watch Mr. Jack Reacher himself Alan Ritchson go toe to toe with an otherworldly threat, War Machine offers up a rapidly paced good time that delivers the exact type of experience it sold to us.

A homecoming for Australian born director Patrick Hughes, who has returned to the land of Oz for his first locally shot film since his noted debut Red Hill in 2010, War Machine is the brainchild of Hughes and writing partner James Beaufort and is the first cab off the rank for Hughes and Beaufort's new production company Huge Film, a partnership that's been established alongside support from Wolf Creek mastermind and fellow Australian filmmaker Greg McLean.

A high-concept offering that shares traces of DNA with sci-fi action classic Predator and at times goes direct into pure horror type territory, War Machine wouldn't feel out of place in a collection of 80's and 90's genre films, as its baggage free delivery of action spectacle, gritty stunts, minimal dialogue and thrills should see it become an instant streaming hit for eager Netflix viewers looking for their next hit of movie-induced adrenaline.

Wasting little time on much in the way of character development or background outside of its main man Ritchson, here playing the grizzled, physically and emotionally wounded 81, who will seemingly stop at nothing to be accepted into the Army Rangers, War Machine starts with blaze of momentum and relentless energy and while there are moments throughout its breakneck 100 or so minutes of running time that don't always work, Ritchson and Hughes ensure the next enjoyable moment isn't too far away.

Built around practical effects where possible and a clearly in the elements ensemble that were put through their paces filming in rural Australia and trying conditions in New Zealand, War Machine's in the trenches approach to getting its vision onto screen is a massive win for the film and in turn a huge boost for the Australian film industry, that is here showcased in a way that hopefully continues to open doors for big-scale international productions that see Australia as a viable country for the movie making universe.

At the core of all of Hughes grand vision here is Ritchson, who gets his best chance yet to lead a feature film after years of notable supporting turns and his breakout Reacher show for Amazon.

Becoming both artistic muses and close friends before and during the process, Hughes asked a lot of Ritchson as the anchor of his film and while he may utter few words throughout War Machine's runtime, it's hard to imagine a more willing leading man than Ritchson who throws himself headfirst into his turn as 81, a man who must overcome present and past demons (both figuratively and literally) to survive an Army Rangers training mission gone horribly wrong.

Proving once more that he's at home when a moment of levity is required but equally as comfortable diving into dangerous rapids or confronting many a moment of physical extremities, Ritchson is having a lot of fun here pushing himself to his limits and with their next collaboration already underway on Australian soil, an as of yet untitled Vietnam set feature, you get a sense that Hughes and Ritchson will continue to grow in a partnership we can all hope is a fruitful one.

It's not to say there's not gold to be found from some of the films supporting cast, made mostly up of local talent, with Blake Richardson as 15 and Alex King as 44 both making their marks, but War Machine really is the Ritchson show and he was more than up for the task.

Entertaining, at times visually spectacular and the perfect type of streaming orientated blockbuster that holds all the big screen virtues filmgoers love, War Machine is the ideal film to turn your brain off too, allowing you to sit back and enjoy the ride without a care in the world.

Final Say -

A big, bold and brash Australian film that never once feels like one, matching it with the giants of Hollywood, War Machine may be simplistic in nature and devoid of any deep depth but that doesn't stop it from being a fantastically fun first up collaboration between Patrick Hughes and Alan Ritchson.

3 1/2 bottles of Stop Leak out of 5.

raw_payload.author: FiftyTwo_52
raw_payload.content:
War Machine feels very much like a poor cousin of Predator. The setup is familiar: a group of soldiers facing an alien threat in hostile terrain. It's watchable, but never really rises above that basic idea.

The film works best if you treat it as casual background entertainment. It's the kind of movie you can watch with popcorn while also glancing at WhatsApp or email without missing much. The pacing keeps things moving, but the story rarely does anything surprising.

Alan Ritchson carries the film reasonably well and brings the same rugged screen presence that fans might recognize from Reacher. Still, nothing here leaves a lasting impression.

It's not terrible - just a typical streaming action film that delivers basic entertainment and little more. Expect something watchable, but also very forgettable.

raw_payload.author: daisukereds
raw_payload.content:
An isolated group of Soldiers face an unknown threat while on their final test for entry into US Ranger regiment. They must do whatever they can to survive and reach their base.

Make no mistake, this is a very familiar premise (very much like Predator).. but for a Netflix entry, it is quite competent and enjoyable. The compact setup takes around half an hour, and delivers a very efficient start to the Action.

The cast is good for what they are asked to deliver, and Alan Ritchson delivers a rigid character whose objective is to fulfilling a promise and never give up, while having a decent arc. After his role Playdate (2025), for which I was quite disappointed, I was reminded as to why I like him. He plays believable hero-man very well.

The visual design of the enemy is quite interesting (a "Metal Gear"), cool and very effective. Cinematography is fine, some not-perfect CGI moments and great sound design.

I might even rewatch this in ..10 years or so. Good enough to recommend! And at no point did it give me anything to laugh about (at it) or feel annoyed.. which is a feat.

raw_payload.author: MovieIQTest
raw_payload.content: I actually like this movie a lot. The only thing that bothered me during watching is the supporting cast. After hurdled so many training harsh obstacles and so many young soldiers were rejected, how could it possibly turn out the final pre-qualified rangers were just some baby-faced, even feminine-looking, or scare-all-the-time weak guys, or wise-cracking team member, or some very vague persons? The only serious characters were only the leading protagonist and the wounded black guy. It's just so ridiculously unconvincing. This is one of the worst casting job that I have ever encountered. But if you could let go of such funny unbelievable feeling about the inappropriate cast, the heavily CGI developed movie itself is still a quite thrilling ride from the very beginning to the end.

raw_payload.author: inc-133-70013
raw_payload.content:
A very vanilla ice cream of a flick, as far as action movies go.

There's angry, yet quite dull space robot, bunch of Ranger cadets without actual weapons, and Alan Ritchson with personal war trauma.

Does that sound exciting? Not incredibly, and turns out, package contains what label says. There's some fun scenes, but it's all obviously very CGI and intentional camera shake is everywhere, trying to hide this fact (doesn't help much).

There's also patriotic saluting and bowing to Rangers that's so sickeningly overdone that I can't even remember any - of the usual suspects - Marines-focused movie to do that. I don't think even North Korea can reach propaganda level of this. Even weirder that it happens in a 2026 movie, not post-9-11 one.

In the end, "War Machine" basically nails its own coffin, because "greatest moment" is Alan Ritchson treating an advanced alien robot like a radiator on a Humvee...

I watched it to the end, but about 70% of entire thing can be easily skipped and you won't lose anything. A background movie while you're doing something else, at best.

raw_payload.author: stevehallcharles
raw_payload.content:
I get it. Films these days are either rated a 1, 5 or 10. Viewers expect every film to be perfect, and perfect for them. This one gets slated for reasons I don't quite get.

It's not perfect. Yes, it copies a lot of of films and the conceit is old. But given that so many films are made, this is going to happen a lot.

There are weak points in the film, and some are rushed and a little formulaic- but it's not too long, and there are no slow boring padding bits. There are comments about them not acting like real hardened soldiers and looking immature and inexperienced? Well, IIRC, they are in training! You wouldn't expect them to be perfect!

Personally, I quite liked it, and was very similar to Battle Los Angeles and I missed there not being a follow up. Maybe this might get one.

Overall, it's definitely worth a try, and it won't amaze you, but it'll entertain you for a bit.

raw_payload.author: senzafine-18333
raw_payload.content:
I enjoy Alan Ritchson's movies and series, he brings some heart and energy to this film, despite a been there, seen that popcorn flick. Well filmed, great action and good CGI ROBOT... but still, it's just ROBOT. That being said, this movie is filled with references to older classic sci-fi. Very little new here and as others have said, you can see scenes coming a mile away.

It's not horrible, but the moments of getting really engaged in the flick is few. I love sci-fi, so today's new sci-fi has to bring the story, not just rehashed flash and bang. It's a rare thing lately a good sci-fi is made... this ain't it. Decent one night watch, but forgettable.

raw_payload.author: redwoodsteve
raw_payload.content: It was a good film right upto the point where the 'Rangers' became 6 year old toddlers with no clue what to do in the face of an unknown enemy, except to stand at stare and then watch as it kills their teammates one by one. And their answer was to scream and cry, like toddlers, not knowing what to do. Disappointing.

raw_payload.author: Jsv94
raw_payload.content:
Fast paced and action packed, but too flawed to call it a hit. If this is the beginning of a franchise, it's going to need better writing to succeed. The amount of violence and gore felt out of place alongside the cheesy one-liners and emotional threads. It was like a horror movie trying to appeal to families. If this were something like Hacksaw Ridge, where a true story is the basis of the plot, it might have worked, but for a fantasy film it just felt like two incompatible genres being forced together.

Props to some of the performances, but because of how the film progresses, there's no character development outside of Alan Ritchson. The biggest gripe I have is with how poorly trained this group of "elite" soldiers comes across. If a giant unknown machine starts scanning you, maybe take cover before it finishes. The lack of survival instinct was more comical than dramatic.

It's definitely not the worst movie, but far from the best. I'll take it over Ice Cube's War of the Worlds, though that's not exactly a high bar to clear.

raw_payload.author: jared-25331
raw_payload.content:
War Machine (2026) is a science fiction action movie on Netflix that was written and directed by Patrick Hughes and it follows the final recruits of a grueling special ops boot camp who encounter a deadly force from beyond this world. This is a movie that I went in wanting to be entertained for 106 minutes and that is exactly what this movie is.

Positives for War Machine (2026): The premise is simple as having a special ops team fighting for their lives against alien threats that is meant to entertain you. There isn't a lot of story to be had, but that is okay with me because I had fun with this movie. Alan Ritchson is great as the main character and he has an arc that is cliched, but I like Ritchson as an actor, so it was easy to root for his character. The movie has a nice mix of having practical and digital effects that look really good. The action sequences are quite brutal and over the top enough to keep you watching. The alien threat looks good enough with the design and CGI. And finally, the ending closes out the story and main arc for Ritchson's character on an nice emotional level that was satisfying for me.

Mixed for War Machine (2026): The pacing gots messy in the final 30 minutes and it dragged on for a bit too long.

Overall, War Machine (2026) is definitely one of the more entertaining action movies from Netflix that you can enjoy with your brain turned off.

raw_payload.author: fung0
raw_payload.content:
Do not be misled by the low reviews. And do not mistake War Machine for one of those zero-budget amateurish direct-to-streaming productions. This is a highly competent, highly entertaining piece of work.

Yes, the formula is familiar. War Machine has echoes of Terminator, Predator and even Walter Hill's classic Southern Comfort. But the mix is new, the script is snappy and the production is first-rate. Definitely worth a watch - or two.

Alan Ritchson has done some great work in the past. He was superb in the sadly under-rated series Titans, playing the dense but well-meaning Hawk. He was less well-served by the scripts in the Jack Reacher series, but he remained likable and watchable.

In War Machine, Ritchson is well cast as an aspiring US Ranger with nagging guilt problems. The action starts slow, but builds steadily - and when it gets going, the movie turns into quite a wild ride.

Outdoor mountain settings really help with the feeling of authenticity. So do the details of Ranger armament and tactics - and even vehicle maintenance. It all feels real enough and smart enough to be properly immersive.

In short, if you enjoy a big action movie with a Sci-Fi twist (more than you enjoy nit-picking) you WILL enjoy War Machine.

raw_payload.content: Take something like Predator meets Robocop, add random soldier flick of someone after a trauma, military sauce and I must admit really good effects. raw_payload.author: jdarragh7

raw_payload.author: Muromets
raw_payload.content:

As for the movie... Well, this is a mediocre (Netflix as usual) cocktail of jingoism and heroic-hero-with-trauma story mixed with Aliens and Predator. CGI is decent, action is OK, and mr. Ritchson is a capable actor, that can show emotions and vulnerability when needed. Protagonist's story is the epitome of banality (as well as the move itself), but actor doing his best with poor material given. This film can be watched. Once. Here, the highest praise possible.

P.s.

In the end the news anchor wonders, why would aliens attack us without reason or provocation. Is it not obvious? They attacked us before humans attacked them first. Because humans were clearly preparing to do so. Yup. Deus vult.

raw_payload.author: ghatbkk
raw_payload.content: Basically this is Independence Day with the focus on a RASP candidate rather than a fighter pilot. So, there are no plot twists or surprises, it's just another rehash of an old plot. But in terms of its focus on a RASP candidate? It's horrible. Rangers are among the best trained light Infantry in the world - yet here we have the best candidates from a selection, some of which have combat experience, doing really dumb things. Standing in the open, failing to take cover, running when taking artillery fire. Plus Dennis Quaid is way too old to be the SGM for the Regiment.

raw_payload.author: ropelawshiel
raw_payload.content: B-movie fun, lots of action. If you enjoy army and war films with a science fiction edge, you will enjoy this. You must look past all the tropes and plot holes and switch off any brain activity, and it's a fun romp. In 1996, another excellent B-movie hit the cinemas: Independence Day, a much better action film that has all this with a lot more fun and emotion. I feel War Machine is a rather poor clone, but it passed an hour and a half. It even has the uplifting speech.

raw_payload.author: Xavier_Stone
raw_payload.content:
I have learned that this movie didn't have any military advisors attached to it and it makes sense. There are so many terrible points in this movie that nobody is going to admit they worked on it.

The writer/director has Expendables 3 to his credit and that's it besides some shorts. It tracks. Zero thinking, no logic.

Patrick Hughes just watched the original Predator movie and decided to remake it. Alan is a decent actor, but he can't carry an entire film.

First off we are in the middle of a special forces selection test, in the final week and are left with 20 scrawny kids from over a hundred candidates. Whining 8 year old boys is a casting fail.

So many plot armor points that it gets annoying to watch. Robot is close, then far away so we can talk, then close again.

Alien robot with unlimited weapons that are lethal sometimes, and firecrackers at other times.

And of course the absolute trash ending of defeating the big boss with a simple trick that only someone with a two digit IQ could say is clever.

2/10 as it could have been good if the script was proof read by someone with tactical experience. Gawd Awful really.

raw_payload.author: thebricks
raw_payload.content:
Very easy to spot this was Pentagon propaganda. Turned me against the film. It's basically Battle LA all over again, using an alien instead of an actual enemy so as to not offend any allies or international powers. The film glorifies the Army Rangers and has a pretty formulaic plot that's been recycled several times, troubled soldier, skeptical commander, said trouble soldier has the key to defeating the alien, soldier is loyal to the Army but endures great sacrifice to achieve victory.

Especially in these times, can't recommend.

raw_payload.author: erikjluna
raw_payload.content: Look, I,realized it was gonna be bad when I saw how they did RASP. But in total, the movie was a solid action flick. I'm glad to see more titles by Ritchson, been a fan since BMS. If you're prior or current service, don't expect accuracy. My eyes rolled so much I felt like a slot machine. But if you want action, you're in the right spot.

raw_payload.author: HOLYDIVER575
raw_payload.content: This film was visually way better than your average Netflix movie. Lots of action in this run from the bad guy film. You believe Alan Ritchson is a military superhero. The action is fast and there is never a pause or slowdown throughout the film. A truly solid film from start to finish. This film will be worth your time.

raw_payload.author: dreamwalk1986
raw_payload.content: As stated in the title this seems just like a recruitment advert for the US ARMY/RANGERS, the film in it's entirety is super cheesy and unrealistic in any way (and I'm a veteran) the whole thing is cringe, cheese with bad acting and passable special effects, borderline no story and what is there is predictable in the first 5 mins, for an actually good film similar to this with probably not even 10% of the budget go and watch "Kill Command", it's better in every way.

raw_payload.author: sotasmith
raw_payload.content: Drivel. Reminiscent of the US Marine recruiting commercial where the guy fights a dragon with an officers sword. LAME. Script seems written by an adolescent who has watched too many bad military movies, monster truck rallies, and has testorone fueled mechanic power fantasies . Remember Michael Bay's "Battleship"? raw_payload.author: TomatoesareRotten
raw_payload.content:

raw_payload.author: iemand-anders1
raw_payload.content:
And even then, its challenging.

Im European, but i learned a lot about this movie.

Elite rangers are apparently a tier 1 unit.

A regiment completely in charge by 70 year old staff sergeants who prefer their most potent candidate to fail the to succeed.

A unit so well trained and feared they stare like bambi in headlights the moment there is danger.

Aliens so advance to travel across universes have the accuracy of about 2%

and so so so much more.

raw_payload.author: markwha
raw_payload.content:
Action? Lots of it.

Gore? Old school and much appreciated

Suspense? Yeah, for sure

Story? Serviceable

Cliches, tropes and cheeseball moments? GALORE

As the title states, this film is basically a mashup of classics like War of the Worlds (Oh..Tom Cruze as Jack Reacher...remember that?) and Predator, then given the pace and flow of a Call Of Duty Campaign.

Once it establishes itself as an all American Military hero propaganda piece (shamelessly I might add) you're able to sit back and enjoy yourself whilst having a pretty good idea of what to expect for the next 105 minutes.

While the tropes, cliches and cheeseball moments will be enough to deter some people, especially critics who are looking for a deeper thinking piece of work, I found it quite refreshing to have a film like this come along.

At the end of the day, the nonstop action, Rated R level gore and sci-fi attributes and very nice cinematography was enough to garner a rating somewhere in the high 60's or low 70's from me. Sure I would have liked a little more submersible story than what we got but there's room for that in potential sequels which I'll welcome with open arms.

7/10, lots of fun and would definitely recommend.

raw_payload.author: stevewalker-10182
raw_payload.content: I didn't expect anything more than a routine battle film and it delivers on that score. The issue I have is: We are told that Rangers are an elite fighting force but this lot seem to have no idea, no effective recon, standing like statues while assessing a potential threat and staying in a group while in open ground. It makes no difference who your enemy is even in a war game you don't wait for proof of hostility.

raw_payload.author: ginagee61
raw_payload.content:
Until the last 15 mins. Loved all the Ranger stuff, the alien stuff and the spectacular special effects. Honestly, there were times when I was quite tense. But honestly an alien that can be stopped by stuffing up the vents?

As for all the bluff, crass OTT terrible writing, etc. Of the last 15 minutes, the less said, the better!!! What on earth were the film-makets thinking? It was very bad, so do yourself a favour and stop watching when 81 gets 7 to the Ranger line.

raw_payload.author: harishnj
raw_payload.content:
War Machine is a disappointing addition to the Netflix action library that feels more like a collection of tired tropes than an original movie. Starring Alan Ritchson, the film attempts to blend military grit with sci-fi elements but fails to make either side of the story truly engaging or interesting.

The plot follows a group of Army Rangers whose training mission is interrupted by a mysterious robotic threat. While this sounds exciting, the execution is flat and predictable. The story moves at a sluggish pace, and the "unimaginable threat" often feels like something we have seen better in movies like Predator or Transformers.

One of the biggest letdowns is the character work. Despite having a talented cast including Dennis Quaid and Stephan James, the characters are one dimensional and lack any real development. Even Ritchson, who is usually very charismatic, is stuck playing a brooding, uninteresting lead. By the time the final credits roll, it is hard to care about the outcome of the mission. It is a forgettable experience that lacks the heart and excitement needed to stand out.

raw_payload.author: AlexKrenshaw
raw_payload.content:
War Machine is the kind of film that works best when you ignore the critics and simply watch it for what it is. This is a proper Saturday night movie. It delivers solid action, impressive special effects, and a pace that keeps things entertaining from start to finish. The story doesn't try to be overly complicated or preachy. Instead, it leans into spectacle and character moments that make the whole thing easy to enjoy.

The action sequences are well done and visually engaging, and the production value is stronger than many people give it credit for. There's a sense of fun running through the film that makes it perfect when you just want to switch off and watch something entertaining.

Not every movie needs to be a masterpiece. Sometimes you just want good action, good effects, and a film that keeps you entertained for a couple of hours. On that level, War Machine does exactly what it sets out to do. Just sit back, relax, and enjoy the ride.

raw_payload.author: wellthatswhatithinkanyway
raw_payload.content:
STAR RATING: ***** Brilliant **** Very Good *** Okay ** Poor * Awful

In honour of his younger brother, whose life he failed to save in Afghanistan, an unnamed staff sergeant joins the Army Rangers, the renowned elite army unit. Assigned number 18, he and his unit must pass a rigorous training test, along with his fellow recruits, in a forest, only for them to suddenly come under attack from a mysterious, giant robotic killing machine, which leaves them in a desperate battle for survival.

Another big budget Netflix production arrives with little publicity or fanfare, which I checked out in lieu of anything else appealing. With the outward appearance of a video game (that I've never heard of) adaptation, with stars I've never heard of (fittingly playing characters with no names!), writer/director Patrick Hughes appears to have delivered just a standard sci-fi action flick, abound with cliches.

There has been speculation that Netflix has been splurging out AI generated scripts in recent times, and War Machine seems to fit into that category, a low grade Predator knock-off with hackneyed dialogue, characters you'll care nothing for and a story with no inspiration or spark. But there is a cool giant robot that pops up every now and again, that ramps up the action sequences somewhat and elevates things.

If you're in the most undemanding of moods, or are such a gamer a video-game-come-to-life would appeal to you, it may be a memorable experience for you, but if you want something deeper, look elsewhere. **

raw_payload.author: akaslammin
raw_payload.content:
A movie about robots, by robots for robots. Because who else would appreciate the soulless spectacle of a script churned out by AI?

This is the inevitable result when you let AI loose on a movie script with the prompt: "Write a movie script that uses elements of high profile 1980s war and alien action movies. It should use hyped action, large explosions, have lasers and involve some MacGyver stuff. Make sure it is not realistic. Use popular video games as a reference for creating the emotional appeal. Treat the audience as if they are robots who prefer not to have to think. The plot, dialogue and characters and how they react should suggest soldiers, just like robots, do not require the ability to think independently. Assume funding for the movie is provided by army recruitment. Oh yeah and chuck something in there about the main character having a brother who dies and redemption or something".

raw_payload.author: rsridhar2005

Hero with a burdened past, hell bent on completing his mission and an all powerful enemy, there to annihilate the hero.

Locales are good. Supporting cast sucks. Music is jarring and photography is topnotch.

The director (Patrick Hughes) never loses his tempo. But, the length of the movie is somewhat dragging towards the end.

Overall, a movie for the weekend. You can simply pass your time, cursing the repeated scenes and the storyline, nevertheless.

raw_payload.author: sami2005
raw_payload.content: War Machine is a film that feels incomplete and underdeveloped from a storytelling perspective. The plot lacks cohesion and depth, making it difficult to stay fully invested in the characters or the events. The only aspect that stands out is the film's strong sound design and large-scale battle sequences, which are technically impressive and visually loud. However, these elements cannot hide the fact that the story itself is repetitive and exaggerated. Much of what the movie presents feels familiar and recycled from countless action and war films before it. In the end, War Machine looks big but offers little substance.

raw_payload.author: Scar38

The only reason I watched this was because Alan Ritchson is in it, he is a brilliant actor, and despite the silly bad reviews I watched it, and I really enjoyed it, yes some of the dialogue was a bit childish in my opinion, but that didn't stop me enjoying it, I turned off my brain and I was glued to the screen for the whole film.

raw_payload.author: LetsReviewThat26

raw_payload.author: Miguel1226
raw_payload.content:
War Machine is an entertaining sci-fi action thriller that succeeds mainly because of its strong pacing.

The film blends a military survival setup with a sci-fi threat, creating a tense and energetic experience once the action begins. While the story and characters are fairly straightforward, the movie keeps things moving at a steady pace and delivers enough suspense and action to stay engaging.

It may not break new ground in the genre, but it works well as a fast-paced and enjoyable action film.

raw_payload.author: AshleyO-699
raw_payload.content:
Honestly my expectations were little to none for this movie, largely in part because Netflix pushed it, and recently their content has been poor. To my suprise this is actually the best feature they have delivered on in a long while, and within this picture there are glimpses of what could of been great, but it falls short of the mark.

I read one review which described this as Lone Solider meets Predator, I couldn't say it better myself. But it's a decent action movie, I enjoyed it, but it's clearly at its best within the action sequences. It's the heavily patriotic dialogue with the best killing machine being 'Americans' being the overall message, and that gets pretty tiresome very quickly. I think if it just eased off with the nonsense about 'we the best' every five minutes this picture would be a lot stronger.

Effects are pretty good in places especially in the action sequences and theirs enough explosions to keep everyone happy, I think their could of been a better design on the machine, it tries to be seen as menacing, but it's hard not to envision the Transformers.

But I think it's well cast, Alan Ritchson is believable in his role due in large to his physique, and he's clearly being pushed as a new face of the action genre, and judging from this, he will likely succeed.

But it's extremely fun if not anything else. And sometimes that's enough for me, definitely worth a watch, but I just hope they don't bother with a unnecessary sequel which will almost inevitably be yet another army Vs aliens flick that noone wants to see. I still haven't got over the Independence Day sequel, so I hope it never comes to that id rather not see a sequel at all. Because it is good, but it ain't that good.

raw_payload.author: arsalanweb
raw_payload.content:
War Machine is the kind of movie that doesn't try to be overly complex or philosophical. Instead, it focuses on delivering a straightforward, action-packed military experience, and in that sense, it does a pretty good job. The film leans heavily into the discipline, training, and combat mindset of the rangers, which actually becomes the most interesting part of the movie.

The story itself is simple and predictable, but that's not necessarily a bad thing here. The pacing stays tight, and the action sequences keep things engaging throughout. Rather than building an intricate plot, the film spends more time showing how the rangers operate-their training, teamwork, and battlefield readiness-which gives it a grounded military vibe.

If you're expecting deep storytelling or big twists, this probably isn't that kind of film. But if you just want a solid, action-driven war movie that highlights military discipline and combat training, War Machine delivers enough entertainment to make it worth watching.

raw_payload.author: xdavid

They respond WAY slower than anyone would do in such a situation. They moralize about their emotions when they could literally be dead the next second. The writing is worse than you'd see from a college first semester drama class. Trust me ... save yourself from this travesty.

raw_payload.author: presto-9

Reacher vs Robot. Everything is extremely predictable, you could almost say the dialogue before it's said. The robot is ineffective when it serves the plot, man, it's just bad.

Alllan Ritchson deserves better. I'm wondering if Netflix is capable of producing one good movie, it's almost a given by now that if it's a Netflix exclusive, it might be better to avoid it.

raw_payload.author: Enes_
raw_payload.content: One of the best films I've seen recently. It felt like it lasted an hour and a half and ten minutes. The acting was fantastic. The script was gripping and well-written, and the effects were extraordinary. Making films like this is very expensive, but the result is incredible. I haven't seen a film like this in a long time. Definitely watch it. The lead actor's performance was also outstanding. Overall, I really liked it. I don't know if there will be a sequel, but at the end of the film, he was going back to war. As the commandos say, "The commando opens the way..."

raw_payload.author: ruidanielbarrossss
raw_payload.content:
This one kept reminding me of a couple of films from roughly fifteen years ago, specifically Battleship from 2012 and Battle: Los Angeles 2011. But on a much smaller, more contained scale.

Where those two went for the full alien invasion war epic, War Machine pulls things way back and goes for something closer to a Predator situation.

A small group of soldiers gets hunted by a single, massive extraterrestrial killing machine, in this case a giant mechanical monster, not a biological alien, which is worth pointing out.

It works, mostly. But it shares a lot of DNA with those older films, and honestly with the whole genre. You get the protagonist who has something to prove, a personal tragedy in his past, and the whole arc of a man redeeming himself through combat and military brotherhood. Alan Ritchson plays 81, and he basically feels like a greatest hits version of every character from films like this. Which is not necessarily a bad thing, because Ritchson is genuinely good at it and the man is built like a small tank.

Is it a good film though? In my honest opinion, no it is not particularly original. You will be able to guess most of what happens well before it does.

But it is fun, it moves fast, the action is well staged by the director who clearly knows this territory, and the supporting cast with Dennis Quaid, Stephan James and Jai Courtney gives it more weight than it probably deserves on paper.

If military sci-fi with a lot of explosions is your thing, this is a perfectly enjoyable way to spend an hour and forty-six minutes on Netflix. Just do not go in expecting anything you have not seen before, because you absolutely have...

raw_payload.author: TheMovieSearch
raw_payload.content:
War Machine tries to blend military action with science fiction, following a group of soldiers sent on what is supposed to be a routine training mission. The situation quickly spirals out of control when a massive extraterrestrial machine begins attacking, turning the exercise into a desperate fight for survival. On paper, the concept sounds exciting and full of potential, mixing combat drama with alien invasion spectacle.

Unfortunately, the execution never fully matches the idea. The film moves at a slow pace and often feels dry, lacking the tension and excitement a premise like this should naturally deliver. Moments that should feel intense or thrilling instead come across as flat, leaving the story feeling somewhat underdeveloped.

Alan Ritchson gives a committed performance and initially builds an interesting character, but the development eventually loses direction. As the story progresses, his arc becomes confusing and less impactful, which weakens the emotional connection to the film.

Overall, War Machine has an interesting concept but struggles to maintain momentum. It ends up being an average watch that may entertain once, but it lacks the depth or excitement needed to make it worth revisiting.

raw_payload.author: virindra
raw_payload.content:
81, played by Alan Ritchson, is a U. S. soldier grieving the loss of his younger brother in combat. Instead of processing his grief, he throws himself into a special ops boot camp. During training, he and his team encounter an alien war machine. That's essentially the whole story.

The movie opens with text that looks like it was made with a free Canva account. That's not exactly a promising start. The rest of the film feels like a macho chick flick: men on steroids, overly dramatized deaths and predictable stakes. From the beginning, it's clear which brother will die and which, like 7 (Stephan James), will survive above the streaming water. The writing telegraphs everything. If the story leaves a character unresolved, you can be certain they won't die anytime soon.

Action-wise, the movie leans heavily on recycled ideas. Alien war machines? Check. Soldiers being chased? Seen it in Predator. Ending with a clever sabotage of the enemy? Battleship did it first. The machine itself looks impressive, a steroid-fueled ED-209 from Robocop, but there's zero explanation of where it came from or why it's attacking Earth. The finale, where 81 disables the alien by tampering with its radiator, is absurd. A space radiator functioning on Earth doesn't make sense, but at least it gives him a satisfying way to finish the mission.

In the end, it's a solid enough action flick with loud music and enough spectacle to entertain if you're looking to turn your brain off. The ending delivers the right dopamine hit, especially when the credits roll.

raw_payload.author: jaysmoke
raw_payload.content:
I think the movie managed to get everyone asking the same question: "How the f do we stop this thing?" The director did a great job of keeping the suspense alive right until the very end.

This movie is raw, bloody, and completely unforgiving. Almost everyone dies - except, of course, the protagonist. With that being said, this film is absolutely pumped with action, and I loved every bit of it. From the intense training scenes to the brutal massacre of the squad and that insane tank chase - wow.

I've heard people compare it to The Predator, and I can see the similarities. But don't be fooled - this movie is just as intense, if not even more intense. I might even add that it has a bit more heart as well.

Definitely one of the best films I've seen this year.

raw_payload.author: JoakimM-7

raw_payload.author: scottgmckenzie
raw_payload.content:
It actually surprised me a bit. The acting is better than you'd expect, and the film at least tries to have a plot, which already puts it ahead of a lot of movies in this lane. The story moves along well enough to keep you watching instead of feeling like random scenes stitched together.

Now let's be honest - this isn't Oscar-level writing. The dialogue isn't exactly Shakespeare, and the script mostly exists to move the story along. But at least the filmmakers made an effort to give it some structure.

One thing that stands out is the presence of Alan Ritchson. He brings a level of physical presence and intensity that helps ground the film a bit. Even when the material isn't deep, he has the kind of screen presence that makes scenes more watchable.

raw_payload.author: destroyerwod

I like Alan Ritchson, especially since Reacher. The guy has a good mix of humor, charisma and is built like a tank. Perfect for action movies. When i watched the trailer i was a bit mixed. It looked promising but i wondered how they would fight such a huge robot.

And the movie was ... well it was what i expected. On one side you have some cool sci-fi battles, some survival elements where a group of US military must overcome a huge alien threat, but on the other side the movie fail a few key points.

First the "Rangers in training" themselves. Outside a couple ones, the majority of them are totally forgettable, generic, both in look and persona. Sure "15" does stand out, and the squad leader black guy as well, but the rest kinda looked all the same including women to a point i had no idea who died and who was still alive often.

Even if some of them are obviously for bodycount, like in Predator, you kinda need to make some character memorable. In Predator i can recall every characters easily even the ones that have limited screen time.

Then due to the nature of the treat and how they are not really equiped to face it, the movie is mostly a game of cat and mouse, wich at times really work, but at the same time the "alien" has no persona and is not really interesting. So on one side its very well done, its interesting to follow, Alan Ritson is really good in it, but on the other side you have a feeling the side characters are even less developed than in a slasher horror movie, and in a way you can kinda feel the movie tried to be the new "Independance Day meet Battle Los Angeles meet Predator" in some way and try to cram too much into an hour and 40 minutes.

So i think 7 out of 10 is appropriated. Its a fun watch, but its not a movie that will become a cult classic or anything. It has its flaw but what it does well it does VERY well. Its Saturday Night pop corn entertaining.

raw_payload.author: mypobox-32602
raw_payload.content:
"War Machine" is the cinematic equivalent of watching paint dry-except the paint would probably have more personality. The whole premise boils down to a group of loud, self-important american terrorists stumbling around until they encounter an alien machine and eventually destroy it. That's it. No depth, no clever twist, no moment where you think "wow, that was interesting." It just trudges along following the most predictable, overused action-movie formula imaginable.

Every scene feels like it was assembled from a checklist: generic tough-guy dialogue, random explosions, some half-hearted attempts at tension, and characters so bland you'll forget their names five minutes later. raw_payload.author: IonicBreezeMachine
raw_payload.content:
Two years after losing his brother (Jai Courtney) to an enemy attack while serving in Kandahar, Afghanistan, a veteran known only as 81 (Alan Ritchson) has entered the Ranger Assessment and Selection Program or RASP to join the 75th Ranger Regiment as he and his brother had talked about while serving. While 81 excels in the training exercises and avoids being sent home, the supervisors of the program show concern for his signs of untreated PTSD and avoidance at bonding with the other recruits. Nonetheless, 81 makes it to the final stretch known as "death march" where he will lead a team of fellow recruits on a training mission through the Colorado wilderness to blow up a downed aircraft and reach the extraction point. However, the group instead find a mechanized automated weapon that picks off the group, is nigh indestructible, and not of this earth.

War Machine is the latest film from Netflix and a vehicle for up and coming leading man Alan Ritchson who is fast becoming a household name in no small part thanks to his work on the Reacher series. According to director Patrick Hughes who is credited with the story, Hughes had wanted to tell a story about the last 24 hours of a training mission in the Army Ranger's selection program and was inspired by a nightmare he had about be chased in the woods by a metallic being with lasers to combine the ideas together. War Machine is a very fun "meat and potatoes" actioner that is held aloft by a strong performance by Alan Ritchson, but it is maybe a little too streamlined to the point no one else really gets to make an impression besides him.

During the opening 40 minutes, War Machine focuses very heavily on the intensity of the ranger training save for the introductory scene of Ritchson's set in Afghanistan where he shows great chemistry with his brother (ably played by Jai Courtney who is gone way too soon in my opinion) and if it weren't for the foreshadowing of what's to come in the news reports on TVs you'd sware you were watching something like 2006's The Guardian only with Army Rangers in place of the Coast Guard. The time is well spent in showing the capabilities both strategic and physical so the genre shift is all the more shocking at the 40 minute mark, and once that shift does occur Hughes and company create some memorable and thrilling setpieces that greatly utilize the enviroment and the impressive but not too over designed titular war machine that looks otherworldly but still practical unlike the creatures from 2012's Battleship which tried to do this military/sci-fi mashup but to much more mixed results.

Unfortunately, while War Machine has excellent craft and a terrific lead character and performance in Ritchson, it doesn't really create a memorable ensemble in the same way that something like Predator did back in 1987 or even to a degree Predator 2 did. While Ritchson is a likable presence and is given a relatable character, a lot of the other recruits feel more like placeholders and of handful we followed after being extremely pared down during the first massacre, the only two who make any sort of impression are Jack Patten's 109 as the comic relief conspiracy theorist (giving the movie a 90s flavoring in terms of genre filmmaking) and Stephan James' 7 who's positioned as being egalitarian and supportive and is more construct than character. None of the supporting cast around Ritchson are "bad" because they're only working with what they're given, but the movie is so anchored to Ritchson's character and arc that the other characters don't really get a chance to make the same impression save for Jai Courtney in about 4 minutes of screentime in the opening and never feel like anything more than glorified red shirts.

War Machine is a fun movie, but it's very much a quick afternoon beer and nachos weekend movie that you'll watch once and probably won't think about the next day. The stuff that's good is really good and if you're looking for engaging action and thrilling setpieces it's there, I just wish it created more memorable characters aside from its lead to fill those set pieces.

raw_payload.author: henry8-3

raw_payload.author: stevecallan-83384
raw_payload.content:
Oh my gerd, how many ways is this movie stupid? This is like Battleship, but the Army version. Toss in a little War of the Worlds, and you already know the plot and outcome.

The Ranger assessment stuff is a complete farce. Just so many things not even close to reality. One of the biggest head scratchers - send them on the final "patrol" with blank adapters on their rifles, but give them live explosives/shape charges?

And how many weeks was the course, and everyone still looks like a fat pig? Couple of weeks into (the real one) Ranger School, people are definitely looking a little gaunt from some weight loss. On the other hand, Battleship plot had a mothballed decommissioned battleship somehow miraculously come back to life and all that, so there's that, to be fair.

raw_payload.author: arungeorge13
raw_payload.content:
I did NOT expect Patrick Hughes to make a more-than-decent sci-fi actioner in the 2020s, but here we are. Alan Ritchson does most of the heavy lifting here (literally), while there's some support from the likes of Esai Morales, Dennis Quaid, and Stephan James. Yes, it's essentially a straight-to-streaming Predator knockoff (without the intelligent traits of that movie), but Hughes packs it with plenty of set pieces that somehow make the proceedings gripping. Expect impaled limbs, charred faces, broken bones, and all of that. That doesn't necessarily make the film grittier, though it keeps us on edge as to what the "surviving" members can do to alleviate this "alien robot" situation.

Given the antagonist is a straight-up, transformer-like killing machine with no personality, it's up to the writers and Ritchson to make a compelling story out of his lead character. Fortunately, even with a short "montage"-y flashback, Ritchson makes it work. The generic survival movie score dumbs things down frequently, and there's the obvious plot armour for the protagonist. However, many of the stunts are practical, neatly integrated with CG for the most part (except for a few explosion scenes, including the final one), making this quite an enthralling ride, with the likelihood of a sequel reviving its memory in the near future.

raw_payload.author: barefootstallion
raw_payload.content:
Yeah, and a significant but.

Alan Ritchson was very good in this. He made you believe the suspense, made you feel the horror and near-helplessness the characters were dealing with. The segments of the movie in which he and his team were being chased, was action-suspense done right. I felt myself relating with the characters' dilemma, fearing for them and rooting for them, all at the same time. That part of the movie was very good. It was pure movie magic.

But the beginning of the film, and later, in how they tied it all up? Or tried to? Dennis Quaid and Esai Morales managed with the short time they had on screen, to just about let almost all of the air right out of this film. Their performances were wooden, and their presences on screen were action-flick-fill-in-schlock. That was how bad they were in this. In fact, all I could do was sit and roll my eyes at their performances, thinking the whole time that they were on screen, how much better of movie this would have been without them.

Apart from that, the special effects were quite good. The antagonist creature and/or mechanoid had a lot of presence, and was simultaneously chilling, a monster-trope marvel and very awesome to watch! It made you dread it and be fascinated by it at the same time. Its design and the way it was written and portrayed, was more of that pure movie magic!

So all in all, a solid 6 out of 10, for a great performance from Alan Ritchson and an antagonist monster that stole the show every time it was in a scene. I would have liked to give it more, because Ritchson and the monster were excellent, but the inclusion of Quaid and Morales were pretty bad missteps that took a lot away from what this film could have otherwise been.

Enjoy!

raw_payload.author: hussainwalikha
raw_payload.content:
One thing I gotta mention is the CGI. It actually looked really good. The effects didn't feel cheap or rushed like you sometimes see in these kinds of movies. When the action kicks in, it looks big and pretty intense. The explosions, fights, and all the chaos going on were done really well and made the scenes way more exciting to watch.

There's a good amount of action throughout the movie too, and most of it lands. It's the kind of stuff that keeps you locked in instead of checking your phone every five minutes. Some moments definitely go full Hollywood and get a bit over the top, but honestly that's part of the fun. The movie knows what it is and just goes with it.

What helps a lot is the pacing. The movie doesn't waste a ton of time and just keeps things moving. Even if a scene is a bit dumb or unrealistic, the next action sequence usually makes up for it pretty quickly. So you're not really sitting there overthinking it, you're just enjoying the ride.

And yeah, ratings online can be weird. There are always people who jump in and rate a movie a 1 without even watching it properly. Happens all the time now. Someone hears one bad thing or just decides to hate it and tanks the score. Because of that I honestly don't trust ratings that much anymore.

Overall though, War Machine is just a solid, fun watch. Good action, nice looking effects, and it moves at a good pace. It's not trying to be some super deep movie and that's totally fine. If you're just in the mood for a fun action movie, give it a try.

raw_payload.author: W_L_Campbell
raw_payload.content: Every so often, a sci-fi action film comes along that has just enough heart to keep it from being hokey. "War Machine" is just such a film. Alan Ritchson leads a cast of highly capable cannon fodder in in a surprisingly sensitive role that explores wartime trauma bonds and the legacy of loss during battle. The action sequences are taut and the visual effects are very well executed. It's not a film for the faint of heart, as the battle sequences are visceral and highlight the terror of war, especially against an untested enemy. It won't win any Academy Awards, regardless of how well it was executed, but it is an excellent entry into the already-rich field of war films-even if the enemy isn't human.

raw_payload.author: urthpainter
raw_payload.content:
War Machine is many things: corny, redundant, predictable, and 100% Awesome!

Almost everything about War Machine can be described as Predator light, except the antagonist, that is an over the top, armored mech utilizing the strategy of an overarmed bulldozer.

Enter Alan Richardson's character - #81, army ranger candidate, scarred from extreme trauma while on active duty. His only mission is to complete training for personal reasons. Unfortunately for him and fellow hopeful rangers, an alien invasion interferes with their final test... hijinks ensues.

War Machine script relies on too many coincidental, intersecting story elements to be believable - but within the structure of average action movie? I'd say story is slightly above average. Movie does have a somewhat rushed feel of a project on a schedule, as opposed to any labor of love, but I'm on board for any entertainment first action movie with science fiction elements. Worth mentioning especially violent movie, and while the special effects are excellent, the drama comes off a bit flat without much character development.

War Machine has a little Starship Troopers in it with regard to patriotism and duty - but where as in Starship Troopers it comes of hilarious and entertaining in a future setting, the seriousness of a contemporary War Machine falls flat during these moments.

I could do without the tac on ending to set up a sequel. Just be a good movie, and if a sequel happens? That's what an intro is for.

Fun action movie with solid special effects if perhaps somewhat unoriginal - 7/10.

raw_payload.author: Hammer-Rocks
raw_payload.content:
Alan Ritchson delivers a controlled, emotionally restrained performance, playing a man driven by grief and unfinished business. His physical presence anchors the film, but it's the quieter moments, where determination and vulnerability meet, that give the story its emotional weight. The supporting cast adds authenticity through camaraderie, fear, and the rough humour that comes from shared hardship, making the group dynamic feel lived in and believable.

The pacing is tight, shifting quickly from the grind of training to escalating danger, and the tone blends military realism with a creeping sense of the unknown. The film carries a rugged, Predator adjacent energy without imitation, keeping its secrets close and its tension steady.

Overall, War Machine stands out as a muscular, emotionally grounded showcase for Ritchson, an intense, atmospheric survival story built on resilience, loyalty, and the weight of personal loss.
